We'd like to tell you about a cool feature in AtG: Group Games!
When you play a Group Game you're doing so on the same map as every other Test Group member.
Group Games help the project in many ways: they encourage discussion, simplify bug tracking, and make it easier to differentiate game imbalances from playstyle preferences.

There are 2 [ways to win|WINNING] in AtG:[BLANK-LINE][*] Conquering the [Capital City|CAPITAL] of one of the two [Roman Empire Factions|ROME].[BLANK-LINE][*] Becoming 'Magister Militum' and taking over [Rome|ROME] from within by sending five [Clans|CLAN] to become Roman Legions.[BLANK-LINE]Note that there are two Roman [Factions|FACTION]: the Eastern and Western halves of the previously-whole-in-better-days Roman Empire, and either one can satisfy your [Victory Conditions|WINNING].[BLANK-LINE]Conquering a Roman capital will require locating it on the map and significant military preparation. Although various setbacks will befall the Romans they start off quite strong and can put up quite a fight.[BLANK-LINE]Becoming Magister Militum is more of an economic victory, as to send a [Clan|CLAN] to [Rome|ROME] not only means sending it off for a year but also providing it with expensive, late-game equipment.

History is defined by those who dared to dream. To accomplish anything meaningful you must first make it reality within your own mind. How does one become a king? It begins with that most audacious of thoughts: 'I should be king, shouldn't I?'

The early medieval era was a time when many dreams were born and many dreams died.

A Leader can be Friends with other Leaders, which affects their behavior towards them. Being nice to a Leader's friends can improve your relationship with the Leader, while being mean to them can hurt it.

A Leader can be Friends with other Leaders, which affects their behavior towards them. Being nice to a Leader's friends can improve your relationship with the Leader, while being mean to them can hurt it.

A Leader can be Enemies with other Leaders, which affects their behavior towards them. Being nice to a Leader's enemies can hurt your relationship with the Leader, while being mean to them can help improve it.

A Leader can be Enemies with other Leaders, which affects their behavior towards them. Being nice to a Leader's enemies can hurt your relationship with the Leader, while being mean to them can help improve it.
Each Leader has an 'Approach', which determines their diplomatic behavior. The Approach they choose is based on their [Personality Traits|LEADER-TRAIT].
Each Leader has an 'Approach', which determines their diplomatic behavior. The Approach they choose is based on their [Personality Traits|LEADER-TRAIT].

[Relationship Level|RL] is how much this Leader likes you. A higher Relationship Level score makes him more likely to work with and aid you. Unlike [Influence|INFLUENCE] it is a fixed value based on past decisions you've made.

[Relationship Level|RL] is how much this Leader likes you. A higher Relationship Level score makes him more likely to work with and aid you. Unlike [Influence|INFLUENCE] it is a fixed value based on past decisions you've made.
[Influence|INFLUENCE] is the current amount of 'diplomatic currency' you have to spend with this Leader. Unlike [Relationship Level|RL] it is spent and then disappears.
[Influence|INFLUENCE] is the current amount of 'diplomatic currency' you have to spend with this Leader. Unlike [Relationship Level|RL] it is spent and then disappears.
[Reputation|REPUTATION] is your global 'diplomatic currency' earned in special situations (e.g. refusing a Demand made by another Leader).

[Leverage|LEVERAGE] is the total amount of 'diplomatic currency' you have to spend with this Leader, right now.

Every few years you'll acquire enough Fame for a new group of wandering Luminaries to appear, in hopes of serving as one of your Ministers.
You can appoint 2 Luminaries to be Ministers. While serving in this position they provide a variety of bonuses. Some expect a salary of Treasure to do so.
Now that you have a few
under your belt, let's talk a little bit about the economy of AtG.
Most types of Units require 1,000 Villagers, and also eat 1 Food per turn.
Except for your capital, each Settlement you control consumes Treasure each turn.
These are the 'gifts' that help grease the wheels of government and secure the loyalty of your lieutenants.
These costs are fairly manageable in the early going, but they grow rapidly with the number of Settlements you control.
In addition to training Units, Settlements can also create other Settlements.
The more you have, the more Resources you can collect from a larger area, but don't build so many that you go bankrupt!
The Villagers who live in your Settlements also create Treasure through taxation. The larger a Settlement gets the more cash it produces. So what do you do with that money?
Specialized Professions like the Explorer and Surveyor are in high demand even during the Dark Ages, and aren't willing to work for free.
Early on, growing one or two Settlements nice and big (~6,000 Villagers) is the most reliable way to pay for those salaries.
You can also bring in more Treasure by moving your Settlements onto rivers and coastlines, where they benefit from trade.
Should you ever find a source of Gold or Gems you can kiss your financial troubles goodbye! Well, for a little while, anyways!
If you have enough Treasure, you can recruit them by moving a Unit next to their camp and clicking on the button that appears in the panel at the bottom of the screen.
Mercenaries are weaker than normal troops, but they don't require spending any Villagers and are handy in a pinch.
